content:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

4 min read
Just now

--

For many developers, AI has become an integral part of their daily work. Large language models support us in our coding work on a daily basis.
As a developer who programmers mainly in C++, Qt, and QML, and who uses Qt Creator as my main IDE, I was delighted to hear about the release of a plugin called Qt AI Assistant for Qt Creator, which allows you to integrate a selected LLM with this IDE.
In this article, I’ll try to share my thoughts on it.
If you’re curious about how to integrate the plugin with Qt Creator or how it works from a ‘technical’ point of view, I’d love for you to read this blog post, in which I describe exactly what you need to do, step by step: https://somcosoftware.com/en/blog/qt-ai-assistant-tutorial-and-honest-opinion
Let’s get started.
First impressions
Once the Qt Creator plugin has been installed, it’s time to integrate it with the chosen model. And here comes a pleasant surprise, as the process is incredibly simple.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

You simply select which large language model you want to connect (GPT, Claude, etc.), enter the necessary API keys, and you’re done. If you have trouble finding your API key, check the support/wiki/documentation sites of your LLM provider.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A bonus is that you can use models you are hosting yourself.
Once everything has been set up, you can start using this plugin
Where it helped in real work
The first feature provided by the Qt AI Assistant is code completion. As we write code (whether in Qt or QML), we receive automatic suggestions that complete the code for us before we’ve even written it. This means that, with a single click, we can, for example, finish implementing a function or add an entire visual element in QML.
Of course, auto-completion won’t read our minds 100 per cent of the time and may occasionally suggest the wrong thing, but generally it works very well.
Another feature is the ability to select a section of code and, via the chat window, instruct the LLM what to do with that section of code. For example, we can ask it to

• carry out a refactor

• explain how a particular section works

• write tests

• generate documentation
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Each of these things helps us save minutes, if not hours, of tedious work. For example, we can leave the writing of AI documentation to the AI and focus ourselves on, say, the architecture of the whole
Where it felt shallow
I’ve outlined the advantages, so now it’s time to look at some of the drawbacks.
First and foremost, the biggest drawback is that the Qt AI Assistant is available exclusively as part of the paid version of Qt. Combined with the fact that we still need to have a license from an LLM provider (or invest time and a lot of money in setting up our own system), this isn’t an ideal solution.
Another issue is that, at the moment, this plugin doesn’t seem to offer all that much. Of course, code autocompletion and the ability to have a code snippet processed by an LLM are very useful, but there’s nothing stopping us from achieving almost the same result without using the plugin.
The last thing I found lacking was a separate chat window where I could converse with the LLM at the level of the entire project’s architecture.
But here I know that with the update to Qt Creator version 20, we should get this capability via the ACP protocol.
What should the Qt Company improve?
Now that I’ve set out the pros and cons, it’s time for me to give Qt Company some suggestions on what could be improved ;)
I realise that Qt Company isn’t a charity and needs to raise funds to develop the Qt framework, but I believe that a feature such as the Qt AI Assistant should be available in the open-source version. It would certainly attract many new users, which would help to popularise the framework even further.
Another issue, which is certainly not so straightforward and would require considerable financial investment, would be hosting a dedicated LLM by Qt Company on their server.
By this, I mean a large language model tailored specifically for development in Qt QML and tested on real-life projects.
Of course, these models are available for self-hosting, but not everyone can afford hardware of this calibre. I think quite a few people would even be willing to pay for access to such specialized models over
Summary
To be honest, although there are a few things I’d change, I feel that the Qt AI Assistant is a step in the right direction.
I think AI coding tools will become increasingly popular, and it’s important to provide users with a tool that makes the most of their potential. Who knows, maybe one day we’ll even see a whole Qt AI module ;)
That’s all for today, thanks!
